q: Why is there typically no more than four to five trophic levels in most food chains?
options: Predators at higher levels voluntarily stop eating once they have consumed enough energy
DNA replication errors accumulate at higher trophic levels, causing genetic collapse
Large energy losses (typically ~90%) occur at each trophic transfer as heat from respiration, so insufficient energy remains to support additional trophic levels
Competition between apex predators limits the number of species at the top, capping trophic levels
explanation: Only about 10% of energy is transferred between trophic levels; the rest is lost as heat through cell respiration. After 4-5 levels, available energy is too low to sustain a viable population.
